SOURCE 1: MANDY HARVEY - DEAF SINGER WHO LOST HEARING AT 18
Rating: 10/10
URL: https://www.cnn.com/2018/02/23/health/turning-points-mandy-harvey-deaf-singer-americas-got-talent
Source Type: CNN Feature Interview
Date: February 23, 2018
Author: Natalie Angley

===== STORY ARC =====
Shy child, slightly hard of hearing → Music was outlet, started singing age 4 → Dream: become choir director → Enrolled Colorado State University vocal music education age 18 → Lost residual hearing over 9 months, couldn't understand teachers → Dropped from music program → "I watched myself die" → Deep depression, stopped leaving house, quit living → Age 20: Father asked her to learn new song → "My goal was to fail" to prove it couldn't be done → Sang into visual tuner, dad crying → New chapter begins → First gig: jazz club, 7 people not paying attention → Multiple songs → Full concerts → Tours → Albums → America's Got Talent Season 12 (2017) → Simon Cowell Golden Buzzer → 4th place finalist → Full-time music career

===== KEY EMOTIONAL MOMENTS =====
1. **Identity death**: "I had made a serious mistake of connecting my entire identity with one single dream. And it died. … I watched myself die."

2. **Deep depression**: "Harvey moved back home and fell into a deep depression. She stopped leaving her house and quit living."

3. **Tired of darkness**: "Eventually, I got tired of sitting in the dark by myself and made the decision that this was not going to be the end of my story."

4. **Father's pivotal request**: When she was 20, her dad asked if she would learn a new song to sing. "My goal was to fail. I just wanted to do my best and prove that it couldn't be done so that we could close this chapter."

5. **Breakthrough moment**: "I just closed my eyes, and I let go. … I opened my eyes, and my dad was crying."

6. **Performance anxiety conquered**: "I am not the performing type. I was the one who would cry, throw up or pass out. I realized that growing up, my biggest fear was always losing my hearing. And that happened. So what's the worst that could happen here?"

7. **Critical voice silenced**: "I think performing has become a lot easier without being able to hear because I can't judge myself. There's always that voice in your head that is so critical, and for me, it was crippling. Now that voice is gone."

8. **Simon Cowell Golden Buzzer**: "Getting the golden buzzer (which means you skip the judges' rounds) from Simon Cowell meant the most because it wasn't that he was paying attention to my story. He was paying attention to the music."

===== ESL MULTIPLIERS =====
- Building New Life: Reconstructing entire identity after losing music
- Belonging: Finding connection through performance despite isolation
- Connection Across Differences: Reaching millions through music without hearing
- Overcoming "Not Good Enough": From depression/failure to AGT stage
- Persistence: 40-70 hours/week practice on pronunciation and pitch
- First-Time Courage: First gig performing for 7 inattentive people, pushing through
- Language Barriers (metaphorical): Learning to "speak" music through vibration/visual tuners

===== FULL ARTICLE TEXT =====

Deaf singer on 'America's Got Talent' feels the music
By Natalie Angley, CNN
4 min read
Published 10:43 AM EST, Fri February 23, 2018

Mandy Harvey takes people on a journey when she sings.

"The thing I love about music … you can close your eyes and be transported back to a memory or a dream or a feeling," the 30-year-old said.

Harvey has near-perfect pitch, so it's hard to believe she is deaf.

"I was always a shy child and slightly hard of hearing, so music was my outlet. I started singing in a choir when I was 4 years old," she said.

Harvey, who grew up in south Florida, was born with deformed eustachian tubes. This led to infections, perforated eardrums and surgeries. She was also born with a connective tissue disorder called Ehlers-Danlos syndrome type 3.

"EDS affects my hearing," she said. "It affects my nerves, my joints, my connective tissue, organs, skin. It affects my whole body, and I dislocate a lot."

But Harvey had a dream. She was determined to become a choir director. At 18, she enrolled in the vocal music education program at Colorado State University.

Dream shattered

"Within the first month, I couldn't understand my teachers," she recalled.

Over nine months, Harvey lost her residual hearing. She was dropped from the music program.

"I had made a serious mistake of connecting my entire identity with one single dream. And it died. … I watched myself die."

Harvey moved back home and fell into a deep depression. She stopped leaving her house and quit living.

"Eventually, I got tired of sitting in the dark by myself and made the decision that this was not going to be the end of my story," she said.

When she was 20, her dad asked if she would learn a new song to sing.

"My goal was to fail. I just wanted to do my best and prove that it couldn't be done so that we could close this chapter," she said.

Harvey sang the notes into a visual tuner and waited for the light to turn green.

"I just closed my eyes, and I let go. … I opened my eyes, and my dad was crying."

Finding her voice

It was the beginning of a new chapter for Harvey.

"I am not the performing type. I was the one who would cry, throw up or pass out," she said. "I realized that growing up, my biggest fear was always losing my hearing. And that happened. So what's the worst that could happen here?"

Harvey enrolled in vocal lessons with the goal of becoming a jazz singer. Her first gig was in a jazz club performing in front of seven people, who weren't paying attention, she remembered.

"That pushed me to singing multiple songs at the club, which pushed me to singing full concerts, which pushed me to going on tour and making albums."

Harvey has a fourth album of original music scheduled for release this year.

"I think performing has become a lot easier without being able to hear because I can't judge myself. There's always that voice in your head that is so critical, and for me, it was crippling. Now that voice is gone," she said.

'America's Got Talent'

Harvey was on season 12 of "America's Got Talent" in 2017. She auditioned with a song she wrote called "Try." The video has been seen by millions of people around the world.

"Getting the golden buzzer (which means you skip the judges' rounds) from Simon Cowell meant the most because it wasn't that he was paying attention to my story. He was paying attention to the music."

Harvey made it all the way to the finals and finished in fourth place.

"I want to inspire and motivate other people to take one step forward," she said.

Singing without sound is a lot of work. But Harvey said she wouldn't have it any other way.

"I work 40 to 70 hours a week on pronouncing words correctly and pitch and singing. Most of it starts with a free app on my phone, which is a visual tuner. I use it all day every day and watch myself sing."

She also performs without shoes to feel the band and the beat.

"Performing on stage is a release. … I really want to connect with people and take them on a journey with me."

===== KEY QUOTES =====
"I had made a serious mistake of connecting my entire identity with one single dream. And it died. … I watched myself die."

"She stopped leaving her house and quit living."

"Eventually, I got tired of sitting in the dark by myself and made the decision that this was not going to be the end of my story."

"My goal was to fail. I just wanted to do my best and prove that it couldn't be done."

"I just closed my eyes, and I let go. … I opened my eyes, and my dad was crying."

"I realized that growing up, my biggest fear was always losing my hearing. And that happened. So what's the worst that could happen here?"

"I am not the performing type. I was the one who would cry, throw up or pass out."

"There's always that voice in your head that is so critical, and for me, it was crippling. Now that voice is gone."

"I work 40 to 70 hours a week on pronouncing words correctly and pitch and singing."

===== CONTEXT FROM OTHER SOURCES =====
(NPR, Fox News, Parade interviews provide additional details)

- Born with deformed eustachian tubes causing infections, perforated eardrums
- Ehlers-Danlos syndrome type 3 (connective tissue disorder)
- Performs barefoot to feel drums/bass through floor
- Uses visual tuner apps to watch herself sing and check pitch
- Father Joe suggested playing guitar together, then singing
- "I expected it to be utter crap" when trying to sing again
- Used to have severe performance anxiety: "throw up or blackout"
- First gig: 7 people at jazz club who weren't paying attention
- Published memoir: "Sensing the Rhythm: Finding My Voice in a World Without Sound"
- Received death threats from some in deaf community for promoting "hearing activity"
- Works with Music: Not Impossible project (vibration technology)
- Uses American Sign Language, always sings and signs simultaneously
- Speaking clearly is "taxing mental work" requiring extensive speech therapy
- "There are days when I lose, and I'm depressed and I can't get out of bed"
- Quote: "I'm not a story; I'm a person, and my passion is music"

===== ANALYSIS =====
This source delivers extraordinary emotional depth with Mandy's devastating identity crisis ("I watched myself die"), prolonged isolation (deep depression, stopped leaving house), and transformation through vulnerability. The father-daughter moment is pivotal and intimate - his request, her expectation to fail, his tears when she succeeded. The detail about her "goal was to fail" shows psychological authenticity rarely captured in disability stories. Her conquered performance anxiety (from throwing up/passing out to AGT stage) demonstrates profound internal shift. The "critical voice" being silenced by deafness is a unique psychological insight. Strong ESL resonance through themes of losing everything, rebuilding identity, first-time courage (7 inattentive people), persistence (40-70 hours/week), and finding belonging through music despite isolation. The barefoot detail adds visceral connection to her adaptation method.
